title: Access Control — Role-Based Violations (Coder Accessing Medication Admin)

spl:
index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" earliest=-24h
| lookup epic_user_roles.csv USER_ID OUTPUT hr_role, allowed_activities mvsep="|"
| eval allowed=if(isnull(allowed_activities), "*", allowed_activities)
| where isnotnull(hr_role) AND NOT match(Activity, allowed)
| stats count values(Activity) as bad_activities by USER_ID, hr_role
| sort - count
description: Detects EHR workspace or activity codes inconsistent with HR job role, supporting least-privilege access control for ePHI workflows.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security, HIPAA Security Rule compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes epic and sourcetypes epic:audit, epic:audit`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Refresh role mapping nightly from IAM; (2) tune with clinical float pools; (3) integrate with provisioning QA before go-live. Indexes required: `index=epic`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=epic:audit`, `sourcetype=epic:audit``; Lookups: `epic_user_roles`, `epic_user_roles.csv`.
visualization: Table (user, role, violations), Heatmap (activity vs role). Key fields: `USER_ID`, `allowed`, `bad_activities`, `hr_role`.
knownFalsePositives: PHI access from authorized clinical workflows, billing operations, audit reviews, or BAA partners.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=epic` `sourcetype=epic:audit` (`Activity`, `USER_ID`), `lookup epic_user_roles.csv` (`USER_ID`, `hr_role`, `allowed_activities`).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Refresh role mapping nightly from IAM; (2) tune with clinical float pools; (3) integrate with provisioning QA before go-live.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" earliest=-24h
| lookup epic_user_roles.csv USER_ID OUTPUT hr_role, allowed_activities mvsep="|"
| eval allowed=if(isnull(allowed_activities), "*", allowed_activities)
| where isnotnull(hr_role) AND NOT match(Activity, allowed)
| stats count values(Activity) as bad_activities by USER_ID, hr_role
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Access Control — Role-Based Violations (Coder Accessing Medication Admin) (§164.312(a)(1))** — Detects EHR workspace or activity codes inconsistent with HR job role, supporting least-privilege access control for ePHI workflows.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=epic` `sourcetype=epic:audit` (`Activity`, `USER_ID`), `lookup epic_user_roles.csv` (`USER_ID`, `hr_role`, `allowed_activities`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: epic; **sourcetype**: epic:audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=epic, sourcetype="epic:audit", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **allowed** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(hr_role) AND NOT match(Activity, allowed)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by USER_ID, hr_role** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the EHR audit trail (Epic, Cerner), walk known-good cases from the same workflow class, and confirm index coverage, field extractions, and access permissions match expectations.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (user, role, violations), Heatmap (activity vs role).
grandmaExplanation: We detect EHR workspace or activity codes inconsistent with HR job role, supporting least-privilege access control for ePHI workflows. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
